summary: My dad was originally from Japan, and he even went back there after the war because he felt bitter about what happened in the United States. I was born and raised here, so I didn’t want to go back, but at 16 I had no choice. We spoke Japanese at home sometimes, and I went to Japanese school on Saturdays, which I hated but it helped me learn the language. Japan was always a part of my family’s identity, even though I felt more American.

I was born and raised in the United States, specifically in Hollywood, Los Angeles. My parents worked hard here; my dad ran a fruit and vegetable stand and my mom worked as a domestic. Even though I was American, after Pearl Harbor I suddenly felt like the enemy because I was Japanese. It was a confusing and difficult time, but this country was my home and where I grew up.

Before moving to Los Angeles, my family lived in Seattle where my dad worked at a jewelry store and was a concert violinist. My mom was the piano accompanist. It was a different time and place, but that’s where some of my parents’ musical talents were nurtured. Seattle was part of our early family story before we settled in Hollywood.

We moved to Los Angeles and lived in Hollywood, where my dad opened a fruit and vegetable stand on Sunset Boulevard. My mom worked as a domestic and spoke English well, which helped her get around. I grew up in a mostly Caucasian neighborhood with a few Japanese families, and I felt comfortable playing with kids of all backgrounds. Los Angeles was where my childhood and early life unfolded.

Anna May Wong was a Chinese movie star who lived on my street in Hollywood. It was pretty special to have someone famous like her nearby. I remember her as part of the small Asian community around us, which included a few Japanese families. She was a notable figure in our neighborhood during my childhood.

John Aiso lived a couple of doors up from us in Hollywood. He later became a judge, famously presiding over the O.J. Simpson case. It was nice to have someone so accomplished in our neighborhood, and it showed me that Japanese Americans could succeed despite the challenges we faced. He was part of the community I grew up around.

As a kid growing up in Hollywood, I used to hike up to the Griffith Park Observatory by myself. It was my favorite place to go when I wanted to be alone and enjoy the outdoors. Sometimes I’d also hike up to the Hollywoodland sign. Those hikes were a way for me to escape and explore on my own.

I remember hiking up to the Hollywoodland sign once or twice when I was a kid. It was part of my solo adventures in the Hollywood Hills. There wasn’t much else to do, so I enjoyed being on my own and exploring those iconic places. It was a special part of my childhood in Los Angeles.

I remember the day Pearl Harbor was bombed in 1941. I was out flying model airplanes with my Caucasian friends when someone told us the news. I didn’t even know where Pearl Harbor was at first, but then everything changed. Suddenly, I felt like the enemy because I was Japanese, and school became a difficult place with kids calling me names. It was a terrible and confusing time for me.

My dad blamed President Franklin D. Roosevelt for being hard on Japan with embargoes before the war. After Pearl Harbor, my dad felt bitter and said it was Roosevelt’s policies that led to the conflict. This was something he told me when I was young, and it shaped how I understood the political tensions of the time. Roosevelt’s decisions had a direct impact on our family’s life.

When Executive Order 9066 was issued, my family had to prepare to leave our home and business. My dad had to sell his fruit and vegetable stand, which was very hard for him after all his hard work. My mom was busy sorting and selling things, and we had to pack up quickly. It was a painful and confusing time, and it changed our lives forever.

Our fruit and vegetable stand was located on Sunset Boulevard in Hollywood. That’s where my dad worked hard running the business before we had to leave for the camps. It was part of our daily life and livelihood in Los Angeles. Losing that business was one of the hardest things for my dad.

My uncle lived near the Hollywood Bowl, and when it was time to go to the camps, we had the option to go with him straight to Manzanar. We chose that option to avoid moving twice. The Hollywood Bowl area was part of our community and family connections before the internment. It was a familiar place during a difficult transition.

We were sent to Manzanar internment camp during the war. My family chose to go straight there with my uncle to avoid moving twice. I remember being on a Greyhound bus with the shades pulled down, not knowing why. Life at Manzanar was hard, but it was where we were forced to live because of the war. It was a defining experience for me and my family.

Our group was originally supposed to go to the Santa Anita Assembly Center before Manzanar, but my mom didn’t want to move twice. So we opted to go straight to Manzanar with my uncle’s group. I don’t remember much about Santa Anita because we never stayed there. It was part of the process of forced relocation during the war.

When we were sent to the camps, we took a Greyhound bus to Manzanar. I remember the bus had the shades pulled down so people outside couldn’t see us, and maybe so we couldn’t see out. It was a strange and unsettling experience, being transported like that. Greyhound was part of how we were moved during the internment.

I didn’t mention Hewlett-Packard in my story, so I don’t have any personal memories or connections with that organization. It wasn’t part of my family’s experience or my childhood in Hollywood or during the war. My focus was more on my family, community, and the events we lived through.
